title: Vendor NSI and public U-Boot A733 clock audit

findings.finding: Vendor SDMMC0 still exposes the known v5p3x node: clocks osc24m, pll_periph, pll_periph_2, mmc, ahb, mmc_store, mmc_mbus, mmc_msi_lite; reset rst; req-page-count=<2>; ctl-spec-caps=<0x428>.
findings.decision: No new SDMMC0 property was found beyond already-tested clocks, reset, descriptor page count, and ctl-spec-caps.

findings.finding: Vendor NSI has private topology/master_clks metadata and bus_nsi resets, but the decompiled values do not name an SDMMC0-specific master gate or ID. Earlier H025-H027 tests already exercised the safe NSI/CCU subset and did not fix descriptor fetch.
findings.decision: Do not create a blind NSI patch from opaque vendor-private metadata.

findings.area: public U-Boot A733 MMC clock code
findings.finding: Public U-Boot A733 support adjusts MMC parent clock math for A733 (pll_hz /= 3) and uses A733 module clock offsets at 0xd00 with 0x10 stride. It still uses CPU/PIO transfers, not Linux normal-IDMAC descriptor fetch.
findings.decision: No IDMAC behavior patch is justified from U-Boot clock code.

findings.finding: The Linux diagnostic path already limits A733 f_max to 25 MHz and has tested the vendor-visible SDMMC0 clock/reset/descriptor paths.
findings.decision: Do not retest plain clock-rate or known clock-consumer variants without new evidence.

conclusion: No kernel patch was made. The next behavior patch still needs a named SDMMC0 master/firewall/security bit, maintainer reply, or a source-backed vendor runtime write outside the SDMMC register window.
